findings.title: Symlink follow on hook write could overwrite unintended file
findings.description: The install script uses `cat > "$HOOK_PATH"` which follows symlinks. If `.git/hooks/pre-commit` is a symlink pointing to an arbitrary path, the script will overwrite the symlink target rather than the symlink itself. In a typical developer workflow this is harmless because the actor who can place the symlink already controls the `.git/` directory. However, in shared or containerised build environments where the `.git/` directory is mounted from an untrusted source (e.g. a CI workspace that checks out attacker-controlled code), this could be used to clobber an unrelated file.
findings.impact: File overwrite at an attacker-controlled path in environments where `.git/hooks/` is not fully trusted. Impact is bounded by the permissions of the process running the install script.
findings.recommendation:
Add a symlink check before the write. Example:
```sh
if [ -L "$HOOK_PATH" ]; then
  echo "ERROR: $HOOK_PATH is a symlink. Aborting to prevent unintended file overwrite." >&2
  exit 1
fi
```
Alternatively, use `ln -sf` followed by a temp-file rename pattern (`cat > tmp && mv tmp dest`) to make the write atomic and symlink-safe.

findings.title: gitleaks binary not integrity-verified — PATH hijacking possible
findings.description: The installed hook locates gitleaks via `command -v gitleaks`, relying entirely on the developer's `$PATH` order. A malicious binary named `gitleaks` placed earlier in `$PATH` (e.g. in a compromised project-local `node_modules/.bin/` or a venv) would be executed instead of the real scanner, silently bypassing secret detection. This is an ambient environment trust issue rather than a flaw in the script itself, but it is worth documenting.
findings.impact: Secret scanning silently bypassed if a malicious `gitleaks` shim is present on `$PATH` before the real binary. Secrets could be committed undetected.
findings.recommendation: Document in `docs/local-setup.md` (Task 7) that developers should verify the gitleaks installation source (e.g. `which gitleaks` and checksum against the GitHub release). For higher assurance, pin an absolute path to the gitleaks binary in the hook (e.g. `/usr/local/bin/gitleaks`) and document this expected location.

findings.category: OWASP A09: Security Logging and Monitoring
findings.title: Hook scan results are not persisted to an audit log
findings.description: Gitleaks output goes only to stdout/stderr of the committing terminal. There is no persistent log of when scans ran, what was detected, or which commits were blocked. In a team environment this makes it impossible to audit whether all developers have the hook active and whether any scan findings were suppressed.
findings.impact: No audit trail for security scanning activity. If a secret is later found in git history, there is no record of whether the hook was active or bypassed at commit time.
findings.recommendation: Consider appending gitleaks output to a local log file (ignored by git) and/or enforcing the hook via a server-side CI check (e.g. GitHub Actions gitleaks scan on push) as a compensating control. The CI gate is more reliable than a client-side hook because it cannot be bypassed with `git commit --no-verify`.
